english: of stupefaction or insanity. 3. frenzy, madness. 4. symb. num.: 13.

insane, frantic, mad, la-dág-pa nyón-pa (la dwags pa nyon pa) a madman from Ladak; gláṅ-po-c̀e (glang po che) Dzl. a mad elephant, ǩyí (khyi) a mad dog; *nyón-pa c̀o dug* (nyon pa cho dug) W. he raves, he is stark mad; *c̀ọ-nyón ẑug* (cho nyon zhug) W. he has been seized with religious insanity, is deranged, which is stated to be occasionally the effect of severe and long continued meditation. Cf. lhóṅ (lhong).

sometimes སྨོ་བ་ smó-ba (smo ba), also སྨར་བ་ smár-ba (smar ba), pf. smras (smras), imp. smros (smros) 1. to speak, to talk, smra ma nús-te (smra ma nus te) Dzl. growing dumb, speechless, not being able to speak (physically); c̀aṅ mi smrá-bar gyúr-to (cang mi smra bar gyur to) they grew speechless, did not know what to say Dzl.; smra śés-nas mir gyúr-to (smra shes nas mir gyur to) they received the faculty of speech and became men Glr.; bslú-baï rnám-pas ǩyêu daṅ smrás-te (bslu ba'i rnam pas khye'u dang smras te) Dzl. speaking to the youth in a seductive manner; tsig snyán-par smrá-ba (tshig snyan par smra ba) Dzl. to speak in a friendly way; c̀ós smrá-ba (chos smra ba) to preach, c̀ós smrá-baï ẑál-la ltá-ba (chos smra ba'i zhal la lta ba) to hang on the preacher's lips, to listen very attentively Pth.; da ma smra ẑig (da ma smra zhig) Dzl. do not lose another word; smra-mǩás(-pa) (smra mkhas (-pa)) speaking shrewdly, well-spoken, eloquent Dzl., Glr.; smra- ̨dód (smra 'dod) talkative, loquacious Cs.; smra-nyúṅ (smra nyung) sparing of words, taciturn, Lt.; smra-bcád (smra bcad) forbearing to speak; not being bound to speak Mil.; smra-mc̀óg (smra mchog), smrá-baï dbaṅ-pyúg (smra ba'i dbang phyug), smrá-baï rgyál-po (smra ba'i rgyal po) = ̨jam-dpál ('jam dpal); also to treat of, with reference to books Was. — 2. to say, mí-la (mi la) to a person; when it precedes the words that are quoted as they were spoken, (the so-called 'oratio obliqua' being very seldom made use of, one instance v. further on): ( ̨di-skad-c̀es) smrás-pa ( ('di skad ces) smras pa) or smrás-so (smras so); when placed after the words spoken, (c̀es) smrás-so ( (ces) smras so), smrás-te (smras te) etc.; smrás-pa (smras pa) also is equivalent to he continued Dzl.; sometimes it is used impersonally, it is said, e.g. it is said in that letter, where we should say, 'that letter says', Stg; smrá-rgyu ma byúṅ-ṅo (smra rgyu ma byung ngo) there remained nothing more for him to say (v. above); rarely with termin. inf.: γtúg-par ni ṅa mi smrao (rtug par ni nga mi smra'o) that they will reach it, I do not pretend to say Thgy.; śés-par smrá-ba (shes par smra ba) to profess to know, to understand, like 'artem profiteri' Dzl.; dṅós-por smrá-ba (dngos por smra ba) to acknowledge a thing in substance Was., med-par smrá-ba (med par smra ba) to deny it in sum and substance. Note. The word which forms the subject of this article, though constantly to be met with in books, seems to be hardly ever used in conversational language.
Cs. word, speech; smraṅ ysól-ba (smrang gsol ba) to beg the word, to beg leave to speak

1. = smrá-ba (smra ba) (?) — 2. to wail, to lament Pth.; more com smre-sṅágs ̨dón-pa (smre sngags 'don pa) to utter lamentations; smre-sṅágs-kyi sgó-nas (smre sngags kyi sgo nas) whining (with joy) Mil. — smre-ytsáṅ (smre gtsang)?

1. the letter sounding ts; tenuis, as in the words 'it got so cold', cf. however ཚ tsá (tsha); ཙ (tsa), ཚ (tsha) and ཛ (dza) represent in Ssk. and Hindi-words the palatals च, छ and ज (ཛྷ (dzha) = झ). - 2. num fig.: 17.
